intent: We help B2B companies grow revenue by generating a consistent flow of high-quality sales meetings through cold outreach and outbound campaigns. Many businesses rely on referrals or inbound and miss the predictability and scale of outbound lead generation. We work with business owners and sales teams to design targeted outbound campaigns that reach the right prospects, improve trust and believability in their marketing, and turn leads into revenue. Our approach focuses on clear messaging, audience segmentation, and a repeatable sales process—including handling objections early, as in the Sandler approach—so you can book more sales calls and scale your pipeline without guessing what works.

I was recently interviewed on the Garlic Marketing Show Podcast by Ian Garlic! 

We discussed How LinkedIn works for B2B Outbound Sales & Cracking the Code to Waking up to Booked Sales Calls Everyday!

Thanks Ian for having me on and hopefully this answers some questions a lot of people ask me in regards to what actually works and some common issues that can hurt or limit your results. 

Enjoy and let me know if I can clarify anything we discussed.  

Link to full YouTube Interview below in the comments...and I definitely  recommend subscribing to Ian's YouTube channel to hear from more successful entrepreneurs about how they are growing their businesses.

Hesitating on Cold OutReach to Grow Your Revenue? 🚀 

Many B2B companies hesitate when it comes to cold outreach and are missing out on massive revenue growth opportunities.

In my experience, they hesitate due to a few reasons: 

1. They get most of their clients via referral and just aren't sure what to do to generate more new opportunities successfully

2. They have tried and failed at cold outreach in the past and that shapes their perception of what's possible with it

3. They generate new business from inbound campaigns or internal sales staff and even though it's not ideally the right quality or volume they either believe nothing else could work better or that they can't afford to invest more into sales/marketing

Alternative Views....

I'll address each of these one by one

1. Getting word of mouth and referral clients is great but it has very little predictability built into it so you never no where your next client will come from or who it will be and it's difficult to scale because your network diminishes over time as you tap it and often times there's limit at which point you've somewhat maximized your network

2. Trying and failing at cold outreach is very common because it's a zero barrier to entry service with global competition so their are a lot of options to try, some of which are great at sales, but have very little experience growing their clients. Look for companies with a proven track record that aren't just good at sales but also have evidence of their success. Even then confirm that they understand the nuances involved in your sales environment to increase the odds that they can build a campaign that would indicate they can competently communicate and target the decision makers you sell. 

3. For the Inbound Campaigns and Sales Staff...It comes down to, are you getting the volume and quality of sales opportunities you want?  If you're getting the volume but not the quality or if you're getting the quality but not the volume then you have a problem that cold outreach can solve. Either way, if you've already got some form of systematic communication with your prospects that's working, then it should be much easier to get cold outreach to work for you because you already have an idea of what your clients want to hear to consider purchasing your services. 

With that baseline, it will be much easier to create success with cold outreach by utilizing those fundamental learnings about your clients.

In my opinion (AND experience), Cold Outreach is the modernized/automated sales person that doesn't take days off or vacations, always does what they're told and reports back to you exactly what's working best so there's NO guessing--Give it and Shot and Get Growing!🥇 

#B2B #b2bmarketingstrategy #ColdOutreach #b2bleadgeneration #salesgrowth 

>>Message me or comment below with any questions 👇

Your Top Sales People are Your Most Trusted Sales People--from the buyers perspective. 💡 

Creating a greater sense of believability in your marketing allows sales people that are not top performers to improve performance because they can gain more trust from your brand by manufacturing trust through marketing.  

⚡ ⚡ What can you do different in your marketing to create a greater degree of trust and enhance your overall sales teams performance? #b2bleadgeneration #b2bsales

a. Was their previous experience selling for a company with an established brand? 

b. Or...were they selling for a company where the brand was not that well established but the value proposition was strong? 

c. Or not an established brand and mediocre to weak value prop and they were still closing sales? 

You can learn a lot from that. #b2bsales #sales 

What unique perspective do you have in regards to evaluating a sales reps past performance when hiring/interviewing?

🚀 Clearing Up Outbound Misconceptions: The Untapped Potential of Outbound Marketing for B2B🎯 

Outbound marketing often gets an unfair reputation, many misunderstand its true benefits. Let's debunk some of the common misconceptions so you can begin taking advantage of one of the top performing biz dev strategies for B2B! 

Myth 1: Outbound marketing doesn't work or is ineffective. 
Truth: While inbound marketing has gained a lot of buzz/press, outbound marketing remains an equally powerful strategy. It enables you to proactively reach your target audience and to hone in on the ideal clients, while also building brand awareness and generate high caliber leads/sales meetings. 

When executed properly, outbound campaigns can deliver exceptional returns with the highest quality sales opportunities. With targeted messaging and precise audience segmentation, outbound marketing enables you to engage with prospects who are more likely to convert into clients. The key lies in identifying the core variables that make or break the campaigns, aligning your marketing strategy with what's working in your business model and optimizing your approach around the feedback/data you receive.

Myth 2: Outbound marketing is only cold calling. 
Truth: Outbound marketing encompasses a wide range of strategies beyond cold calling. Email marketing, direct mail, social media advertising, and even event sponsorships are all forms of outbound. Yes it can operate like a cold call and often does but if you're a relationship builder purist or a PR die hard you can still leverage outbound to tee up those types of opportunities as well. By diversifying your outbound efforts and leveraging multiple channels, you can create a cohesive and impactful campaign that resonates with your style and audience.

Myth 3: Outbound marketing is intrusive and annoys prospects. 
Truth: It's important to approach outbound marketing with respect and relevance. By tailoring your messaging to the needs and interests of your target audience, you can provide value and establish meaningful connections. When done right, outbound marketing helps prospects discover solutions that address their pain points. That being said, all advertising has the potential to annoy someone and outbound is no different, so the key here is to look for patterns to avoid and not get caught up in adjusting an effective campaign over one off piece of negative feedback. If you identify a repeat pattern of offense then you make an adjustment in that one campaign message, category or audience.

🌞 Embracing the untapped potential of outbound marketing can elevate your visibility, generate high quality leads and sales meetings, and create revenue growth. 📈 

It's not an either-or choice between word of mouth/referral, inbound and outbound; they all should work in harmony to create a comprehensive-synergistic marketing strategy. 

Share your thoughts and experiences below. 👇

One of the biggest mistakes I see business owners make is paying other companies for leads or listings without investing anything into creating their own brand online.  

If all your sales opportunities come from other sources outside of your control and ownership a couple things happen...

1. All your sales opportunities come through their marketing messages. Typically this means lower quality and predominately price oriented. (We can talk about why)

2. Your marketing investments have ZERO residual benefit to you. None of the prospects come into direct contact with your brand on the front end of the research process. 

Depending on your industry 85 yo 95 percent of the prospects online are still in the research phase. 

If your only source of new sales opportunities comes from buying leads or directory listings, that means most of the time you're only coming into contact with people at the very end of the research process. 

You have no residual pipeline. You're hooked up to the system. If you don't pay them next month then they shut off your lead flow. 

That's not how your online marketing is supposed to work for your business. 

We have clients that are still generating hundreds of leads per month off of work they paid for us to complete years ago.
This is incredible!

Great advice for all businesses when thinking about their sales process.

Sandler Rule #23
"The way to get rid of a bomb is to defuse it before it blows up."

Have you ever lost a sale because of a problem you could have dealt with earlier on in the process?

If you have the possibility of a reoccurring problem with your product or service, don't expose yourself to the stress of wondering why and how your prospect is going to lower the boom. Bring the issue up before the prospect does. Defuse it!

You:
One of the problems we may have, Harry, assuming that we decide to do business together, is this. We don't provide local service. Is that going to be a problem?

Prospect:
Yes, I'm afraid it may be.

You:
I understand, Harry. Do you want to talk about why it will be an issue for you?

Bringing the problem up yourself makes it easier for you to handle the potential objection, rather than having to handle the problem while you're on the 'defensive'.

If you are open to learning more about Sandler, I encourage you to click the link below and signup for an immediate & complimentary virtual training.

For the love of god... JUST GIVE ME THE PRICE!

I would love to hear Salespeople's thoughts...  How would you improve this?

Nothing frustrates me more than going through a typical SaaS Sales Cycle, let me break a RL example I recently experienced with a SaaS Globalization platform:

Context: 
An SDR from the SaaS firm had been contacting me for months via cold calls, SMS, email, and LinkedIn messaging.   (FYI - I run a business so I never get upset about salespeople doing their job)

I had seen the messages but hadn't felt the need to respond.  However, one sunny Tuesday morning as I was strolling to the office the SDR hit me with a cold call that I answered.  

SDR went straight into her pitch:  
"Hi Kevin, this is X with Y.  We are the leaders in employee globalization and payments.  I presume you have heard of us?"

I love the intro she was confident, got to the point, and didn't waste my time by asking a BS question like "Hey I know I called you out of the blue but do you have a couple of minutes for me to tell you what the calls are about"

My response:
"I appreciate the call and great intro, I do know of Y but we don't have any needs for your service"

SDR:
"Thanks for that.  Many of my clients have been experiencing issues with Z, we have recently launched a new solution that reduces the risk factor by..."

Me:
"OK, that actually could have interest.  What is the pricing for that"

SDR: 
"Great, I would love to set you up with an AE that can go into more detail"

Me:
"I don't need more detail.  I completely get it and it could be valuable for us, I just need the have the price and I will have my HR and Fiance team run an analysis" 

SDR:
"Well, it depends on many factors" - She goes into a Disco

Me:
"I have told you everything you need to know.  Know whats the price"

SDR:
"I don't have authority to discuss that, I need to set you up with my AE"

Me:
"OK, set me up.  But please let them know I just want to discuss pricing, I have given you all the info you need" 

SDR:
"Great, how does next Wednesday work"

Me: 
"That's over a week away?"

SDR:
"Yes, sorry my AE is out on vacation this week"

Me: (Starting to feel like Harry Potter in front of a Dementor)
"OK, fine. Wednesday at 2pm works"


Skip forward... The AE Meeting


AE: Jumps into a massive sales monologue

ME: "Look I get it, please just let me know the pricing" 

AE: "It depends on many factors" 

ME: (deja vu kicking in) I went through this with your SDR

AE: "I know but I would like to go through it again to make sure I have it correct"

ME: 😑 🔫 


Let's skip through 20 mins of my life I will never get back...  Finally:


ME: "OK, you have everything.  What the pricing?"

AE: "Let's book another meeting to go over pricing"

ME: 💀 "Just email me pricing when you figure it out"

AE: "I can't email you, we have to discuss pricing during a meeting"

ME: "OK - Bye" 👋 



Over to you: Thoughts?

#SDR  #AccountExecutive #BrokenSalesProcess

I'm shocked by how few salespeople do this.

The cost? $0.00.

It’s asking prospects who ghosted you for feedback.

Here's how to ask for feedback:

1. Create a list of prospects who have ghosted you (people who expressed interest but didn't show for a meeting).

2. Call them and say this:

"Hi John, it's Lisa with ACME. I know you weren't expecting my call, but I was hoping you could help me out for a moment."

"Thanks. It seems you're {putting the cold call workshop on hold.} I'm not calling to move anything forward, but was wondering if you'd be open to providing some feedback so I can better serve you should a need arise in the future."

3. Then, listen without having an agenda.

When you let go of the sale, prospects lower their guard and tell you the truth because they don't feel pressured.

Why does this matter? 

The more conversations you have with your ideal prospects, the more money you make. 

The opposite is also true.

This works. (Proof video below.)
The question is, will you do it?

More badass plays that start conversations here - https://lnkd.in/e6Z3Y_YA

If I had to start from ZERO ...$0 to $1,000,000 - here's my marketing plan

$0 - $100,000 ARR
Linkedin Organic - 5x a week posting and 30-60 min a day engaging
Youtube channel - at least 4x videos a month
Newsletter - 1x/mo at first
Gumroad - simple free checklists and paid consulting links (basically just some extra cash flow)

I'd create a basic framework of content pillars focused on my niche and then use that content to produce a few posts for blog and videos for Youtube and package that content into a monthly newsletter too. 

On my Linkedin profile, I'd use Linktree to funnel traffic to all the above as well. 

When I get some decent cash flow months, I'd invest in upgrading the website. 

$100,000 to $250,000 ARR 

I'd start tapping into Google and clutch (or G2 if you're saas)

Start light retargeting with Linkedin ads and FB, maybe.

If there is an organic channel that you're getting more traction with , double down on that and dial back something else. 

Strategically you might typically be starting with broader range of services and might be looking to narrow that down a bit and scale deeper what your core offer really is. 

$250,000 - $500,000 ARR

I'd be scaling Google ads, listing sites, investing more heavily in SEO, introducing Linkedin cold ads, and light retargeting on programmatic + CTV

You should have a much better idea now what your clients really want, what your core offer is, and who you work best with. 

Use this info to dial in your marketing channels to attract more of your best fit clients. 

$500,000 to $1,000,000 ARR

We pretty much have the right framework in place here, but I'd be scaling up the different channels and improving the quality of content/video/images/website. 

Go deep on your niche target - build out specialize campaigns, landing pages, and even tailor offers for them. 

$1,000,000 to $10,000,000 ARR

This is where I'd be looking to new areas to test and expand. 

Digging deeper into referral partnerships
Strategic collaborations with complementary businesses
Events
Segmenting and digging deeper into niches
Experimental platforms more like Quora, reddit, twitter, tiktok etc.

This is just the path most comfortable to me - Others in my network are comfortable or more experienced with things like cold calling/emailing, advertising on other channels, or other legitimate levers. 

The path 1 1M or 10M ARR look different for every startup. 

There is one thing I know for sure.....

Don't just use someone else's playbook - make your own based on your unique strengths. 

Linkedin organic might not be the platform you choose to dominate..it might be twitter or tiktok, and that's fine. 

The point is go deep on one organic platform and commit to consistency and engagement and actual sharing of deep expertise. 

The point is....I appreciate you even reading this far 😜 

#linkedinads #marketing #entrepreneurship

This message is for all the givers and empaths navigating spaces and places that do not appreciate and nurture their light. I see you. 

Be careful whom you allow into your space if you are a giver.

Your energy and time are finite. 

Takers will take till you have nothing left to give. 

Having been in corporate America for over two decades, I remember feeling like a line item to the companies I worked for; I was only valuable if I could produce. And sometimes, that wasn’t enough.

It makes me think of the Spencer W Kimball quote, “Love people, not things: Use things, not people.” 

Be careful of the vultures in your personal life and at work. 

Instead, surround yourself with those who are kind, compassionate, and giving. Get rid of the takers and surround yourself with those who want more for you, not from you. 

Let me know if you hear me.

Love,
Lan Phan

I like to read. A lot. For the past 3 years, I have averaged around 60 books a year. I read both fiction and non-fiction, across a lot of genres.

My favorite US President, Harry S. Truman said it best, "Not all readers are leaders, but all leaders are readers."

This week, I thought I'd highlight the top 5 business books I have read in 2022. 

1 - Who Not How by Dan Sullivan and Dr. Benjamin Hardy

This book came highly recommended by a trusted colleague as a catalyst for the way he approaches his life and business. It has been the same for me. The number one question I used to ask when looking at solving a problem was, "how can I do this?" The question I should be asking is, "who can do this for me?" That's not a lazy question, but more of a question about who is an expert or specialist in this field that can do this task or project in less time, with less effort, and produce a better result than I can. This allows me to stay focused on what I am good at and bring others around me who are experts in their areas. My work has enabled me to hire folks as copyrighters, graphic designers, and sales and marketing experts. It has truly been game-changing for me!

Here they are again, the top 5 business books I read in 2022:
5- 3HAG Way by Shannon Byrne Susko
4 - Energy Leadership by Bruce D Schneider
3- Hero on a Mission by Donald Miller
2 - Raving Fans by Ken Blanchard and Sheldon Bowles
1 - Who Not How by Dan Sullivan and Dr. Benjamin Hardy

What would your top 5 list look like?

Russel & Bromley filed for bankruptcy. How sad to see 150 year old UK family owned business come to this. That business survived the 2 great wars but couldn’t survive the high-tax, low growth economy we have today. 

A friend told me today she’s had to move her offices into a new borough to reduce council tax. She’s stopped hiring UK staff due to increased National Insurance tax. She’s discovered she can outsource the jobs to South Africa. 

Another friend shared that he pays himself £100K income and then put’s the other £60K into his pension. The high tax threshold stops him from paying himself over£100K. So that extra £60K goes into the USA S&P500 instead of the UK economy. 

An employee who works for another friend hit £50K income and wants to work 4.5days a week instead of getting a pay rise that would trigger higher tax rates. 

The founder of a £300M AI company left the UK this week because he can’t get the talent to move into high tax Briton. He won’t be paying millions in taxes to the UK. 

The government raised capital gains tax rates last year and collected 8% less than than they collected with the lower rates. 

Singapore has a top rate of tax of 22%, no CGT or IHT at all. They have one of the best run countries on earth - schools, roads, safety, etc. Same for the Swiss. Oh, and they have economic growth!

Can you  spot the trend here? When I push the break pedal in my car it slows down… I can see the cause and the effect. When I put my hand on a hot stove it burns. When governments become big and taxes become high, the economy crumbles.

You can beat 99% of people by:

preparing the night before…
showing up early…
leaving only when the job is done exceptionally right…
remembering people’s names…
following up…
improving one thing each time... 

Do all of this consistently, every day, for one year. 
I promise you will get closer to your goals. 

- Alex ✊🏽

Ten years of business lessons in one post. 

1. You're likely to lose friends.
Going 100mph towards your goals will lose you friends and supporters. But that’s okay—you’ll find better ones at your destination. 

2. Business isn't fair.
You can play fair but don't expect others to follow suit. Some people will not play fair, but they win when their unfairness changes your behavior. If you avoid losing, you also avoid winning.

3. Growth requires growing pains (not joys)
Scaling Gym Launch from $7M to $27M in under 2 years was one of the most painful experiences of my life. Your mind is rarely at peace. And the amount of problems that come up when you scale so quickly is exhausting. Hard times today are us footing the bill for the traits we wish to have tomorrow. 

4. Do not mistake a luxury for a requirement.
People use a lack of motivation, vision, and purpose as excuses not to start. Motivation comes from responsibility, not the other way around. Seek responsibility and motivation will take care of itself.

5. No such thing as imposter syndrome.
There's just inexperience, insecurity, and sucking before you're good. When I was 24 I felt like an impostor leading a team of 120 people. Do something a thousand times and tell me if you still feel like an impostor. 

6. Success doesn't make you feel better.
My first time scaling Gym Launch, I felt constant anxiety and stress about not being good enough. I kept thinking “Once I hit certain levels of success, the pain will go away.” Success doesn't solve these problems—they just sit in a different category.

7. Delegate and reinforce.
When you delegate, you're trading the time spent doing the task for the time it takes to reinforce and reward it. As your business scales, you need to become the Chief Reinforcement Officer. Delegation > Abdication. 

8. Titles don't make leaders, actions do.
Look for those who already have followers in your company. Who do people listen to? That's who I've promoted. You don't make someone a leader by giving them a title—you make official what's already happening. You make what’s obvious, official. 

9. Strategy is saying “no.”
You have to say no to 99.9% of opportunities to say yes to what really moves the needle the most. At Gym Launch, we chose to start a supplement company over international expansion. Even though both companies were successful, I'm certain we'd have been 2-3x more successful focusing on expansion. 

10. When to do something new? 
When you cannot do anything anymore or better, do something new—but be damn sure you've exhausted all options first. Most people underestimate how much juice they can squeeze out of what they're currently doing. If you’re not executing at a 10/10, it’s likely not your strategy that’s the issue. 

If you want to make it easier for yourself, I have assembled a Scaling Roadmap. It shows you the problems to expect going from $0-$100M. It's free. Link in the comments.

12 business lessons my husband taught me
that made me the woman I am today:

1. It’s okay to be weird. Different. Misunderstood. Normal people get normal outcomes - your weirdness is a gift.

2. You’re not bad at anything. You need more reps. Stop judging yourself and do the work.

3. At the end of the day we are a tiny spec within a giant galaxy and when we die nothing matters. So stop putting so much pressure on yourself.

4. Anxiety isn’t bad. Being terrified isn’t bad. Fear isn’t bad. The worst case is you continue to feel bad, but you won’t die.

5. Your life is a story. When you’re faced with hard times ask yourself: "What story do I want to tell about what happens next?"

6. Material things are only valuable if they give you attention back rather than take it.

7. If someone hates you and it bothers you. There’s probably truth to it. And so what?

8. Fight for what you want. You can’t expect people to give it to you just because you’re a good person. Be a good person and ALSO fight for it.

9. There are no rules on how much and when to work. When and how to work out. How to be married. Rules are a substitute for people who don’t have values.

10. You don’t need therapy for everything. Accept life is unbearably painful at times, and that’s okay.

11. Happy people don’t spend their lives TRYING to be happy, they do what they want and happiness is a byproduct.

12. The person that’s capable of saving the day and fixing all your problems, is you.

Thank you for being an example of what a true man is.

This week, the U.S. Senate approved versions of the Kids Online Safety Act and the Children’s Online Privacy Protection Act, which would represent some of the most significant legislation in decades aimed at addressing how platforms can interact with children. Passage in the House remains at best an open question. Some members are already expressing serious reservations. 
 
As someone who works in early childhood education policy and as a parent, I am interested to see what approach, if any, states and the federal government coalesce around to ensure that kids do not experience so many adverse consequences from spending time online. A couple of weeks ago, Kernard D. Jones and I hosted a roundtable on screen usage and mental health focused on early learners at the Education Commission of the States National Forum on Education Policy. One takeaway for us is that there’s a ton of interest and energy to do something, but that we’re still looking for consensus on what that is. 
 
This week’s legislative wrangling is a reminder, though, that as tech platforms and parents, we don’t have to wait. There are things that we can and should do to try to help ensure that any experiences kids do have online are positive and age appropriate. One of the reasons that I’m proud to work at Waterford.org is that we center children in the design of our programs.  Our programs are time limited, highly interactive, age appropriate, rooted in the science of learning, and offered free of advertising. Technology can continue to be an essential tool for helping children learn, but we need to be proactive to ensure that it is.
